description.en: Use all-mpnet-base-v2 embeddings to cluster semantically similar documents, detect near-duplicates, and discover latent topics in text corpora. Covers cosine similarity matrices, agglomerative clustering, and threshold-based deduplication for RAG knowledge bases.

story_variants.en:
## Semantic Clustering & Deduplication with all-mpnet-base-v2

Vaathiyaar here! Your RAG knowledge base has 50,000 document chunks — but how many are actually unique? Duplicate and near-duplicate content wastes vector storage, skews retrieval scores, and floods your LLM context with redundant information. The fix: embed everything with `all-mpnet-base-v2`, build a similarity matrix, and let the embeddings tell you which chunks are semantically identical. Then cluster what remains to discover the natural topics in your corpus.

### Why Deduplication Matters for RAG

When your knowledge base contains three slightly different phrasings of the same policy, your retriever returns all three — burning context tokens on redundant information. Worse, the LLM may produce contradictory answers if the duplicates have minor wording differences. Semantic deduplication catches copies that keyword matching misses.

| Dedup Method | Catches Exact Copies | Catches Paraphrases | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hash-based (MD5) | Yes | No | O(n) |
| Jaccard similarity | Yes | Partial | O(n²) |
| Embedding similarity | Yes | **Yes** | O(n²) but batched |

Embedding-based deduplication with mpnet catches paraphrased duplicates — "The return window is 30 days" and "Items may be returned within thirty days" map to nearby vectors.

### Building a Similarity Matrix

```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

documents = [
    "The return window is 30 days from purchase.",
    "Items may be returned within thirty days.",
    "Shipping takes 5-7 business days.",
    "Delivery typically arrives in 5 to 7 working days.",
    "Contact support at help@example.com.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True)
similarity_matrix = embeddings @ embeddings.T
print(similarity_matrix.round(2))
```

The similarity matrix is a 5×5 grid where entry (i, j) is the cosine similarity between documents i and j. Diagonal entries are always 1.0 (a document is identical to itself). Off-diagonal values above 0.85 signal near-duplicates.

### Threshold-Based Deduplication

```python
def deduplicate(texts, model, threshold=0.85):
    embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
    sim_matrix = embeddings @ embeddings.T
    keep = []
    removed = set()
    for i in range(len(texts)):
        if i in removed:
            continue
        keep.append(i)
        for j in range(i + 1, len(texts)):
            if sim_matrix[i][j] >= threshold:
                removed.add(j)
    return [texts[i] for i in keep], list(removed)

unique_docs, removed_indices = deduplicate(documents, model, threshold=0.85)
for doc in unique_docs:
    print(doc[:60])
```

This greedy algorithm walks through documents in order: keep each document, then mark all sufficiently similar later documents as duplicates. The threshold controls aggressiveness — 0.85 catches paraphrases while keeping distinct content.

### Agglomerative Clustering for Topic Discovery

```python
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering

corpus = [
    "Python list comprehension tutorial",
    "How to use list comprehensions in Python",
    "Introduction to neural networks",
    "Deep learning basics: neural network architecture",
    "REST API design best practices",
    "Building RESTful APIs with Flask",
]

embeddings = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
distance_matrix = 1 - (embeddings @ embeddings.T)

clustering = AgglomerativeClustering(
    n_clusters=3,
    metric="precomputed",
    linkage="average",
)
labels = clustering.fit_predict(distance_matrix)

for cluster_id in sorted(set(labels)):
    members = [corpus[i] for i, l in enumerate(labels) if l == cluster_id]
    print(f"Cluster {cluster_id}: {members}")
```

Agglomerative clustering merges the closest pairs of documents bottom-up until you have the desired number of clusters. Using `1 - cosine_similarity` as the distance metric and `precomputed` mode lets you feed the mpnet similarity matrix directly. The result: automatic topic discovery without any labeled data.

### Combining Dedup + Clustering for RAG Cleanup

```python
def clean_corpus(texts, model, dedup_threshold=0.85, n_clusters=None):
    unique, _ = deduplicate(texts, model, dedup_threshold)
    if n_clusters and len(unique) > n_clusters:
        embs = model.encode(unique, normalize_embeddings=True)
        dist = 1 - (embs @ embs.T)
        labels = AgglomerativeClustering(
            n_clusters=n_clusters, metric="precomputed", linkage="average"
        ).fit_predict(dist)
        return {lbl: [unique[i] for i, l in enumerate(labels) if l == lbl]
                for lbl in sorted(set(labels))}
    return {0: unique}

result = clean_corpus(corpus, model, dedup_threshold=0.90, n_clusters=3)
for topic, docs in result.items():
    print(f"Topic {topic}: {len(docs)} docs")
```

First deduplicate, then cluster. This two-step pipeline removes redundancy and organizes surviving documents by topic — giving you a clean, structured knowledge base for RAG.

> **Key Insight:** Semantic deduplication with all-mpnet-base-v2 catches paraphrased duplicates that hash-based and keyword methods miss. Combine it with agglomerative clustering to both clean and organize your RAG corpus — fewer redundant chunks means better retrieval and less wasted LLM context. A similarity threshold of 0.85 is a strong starting point for most English-language corpora.

animation_sequence.content: Your RAG knowledge base is full of near-duplicates that waste storage and confuse your LLM. Semantic deduplication with all-mpnet-base-v2 catches paraphrased copies that keyword matching misses, then agglomerative clustering organizes what remains into discoverable topics — all without labeled data.

animation_sequence.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")

documents = [
    "The return window is 30 days from purchase.",
    "Items may be returned within thirty days.",
    "Shipping takes 5-7 business days.",
    "Delivery typically arrives in 5 to 7 working days.",
    "Contact support at help@example.com.",
]

embeddings = model.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True)
similarity_matrix = embeddings @ embeddings.T
print(similarity_matrix.shape)  # (5, 5)
print(similarity_matrix[0][1])  # High — near-duplicate
print(similarity_matrix[0][4])  # Low — unrelated

steps.explanation: Import SentenceTransformer — the library that converts variable-length text into fixed-size numeric vectors capturing semantic meaning.
Load all-mpnet-base-v2 — produces 768-dimensional embeddings with the highest STS benchmark score (0.838) among open-source sentence transformers.
Five documents with intentional near-duplicates: docs 0-1 say the same thing about returns, docs 2-3 say the same thing about shipping. Doc 4 is unique.
encode() converts each document to a 768-float vector. normalize_embeddings=True makes each vector unit-length so dot product equals cosine similarity.
Matrix multiply embeddings @ embeddings.T computes all pairwise cosine similarities in one vectorized operation. Result is a 5×5 symmetric matrix.
Shape (5, 5): one row and column per document. Entry (i, j) is the cosine similarity between documents i and j. Diagonal is always 1.0.
similarity_matrix[0][1] will be high (~0.85+) because both documents describe the same 30-day return policy in different words.
similarity_matrix[0][4] will be low (~0.1) because return policies and contact info are semantically unrelated. This contrast is what makes deduplication possible.
The deduplication function takes texts, a model, and a similarity threshold. Documents scoring above the threshold against an already-kept document are marked as duplicates.
Encode all texts at once — batch encoding is much faster than encoding one at a time. Normalized embeddings let us use dot product as similarity.
Build the full pairwise similarity matrix. For N documents this is O(N²) — fine for corpora up to ~100K. Beyond that, use approximate nearest neighbors (FAISS).
Greedy scan: iterate through documents in order. If a document hasn't been removed yet, keep it and check all later documents against it.
Inner loop checks every later document against the current keeper. If similarity meets the threshold, mark it as a duplicate. First-seen wins.
Return two values: the deduplicated text list and the indices of removed documents. The caller can log what was removed for auditing.
Run deduplication on our 5 documents. With threshold 0.85, the two return-policy docs collapse to one, and the two shipping docs collapse to one — leaving 3 unique documents.

animation_sequence.code:
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering

corpus = [
    "Python list comprehension tutorial",
    "How to use list comprehensions in Python",
    "Introduction to neural networks",
    "Deep learning basics: neural network architecture",
    "REST API design best practices",
    "Building RESTful APIs with Flask",
]

embeddings = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
distance_matrix = 1 - (embeddings @ embeddings.T)

clustering = AgglomerativeClustering(
    n_clusters=3,
    metric="precomputed",
    linkage="average",
)
labels = clustering.fit_predict(distance_matrix)

for cluster_id in sorted(set(labels)):
    members = [corpus[i] for i, l in enumerate(labels) if l == cluster_id]
    print(f"Cluster {cluster_id}: {members}")

steps.explanation: AgglomerativeClustering from scikit-learn performs bottom-up hierarchical clustering — it starts with each document as its own cluster and merges the closest pairs.
Six documents spanning three topics: Python lists, neural networks, and REST APIs. Each topic has two differently-worded documents.
Encode all documents with mpnet. The 768-dimensional embeddings capture semantic meaning — documents about the same topic will have nearby vectors.
Convert similarity to distance: distance = 1 - cosine_similarity. Clustering algorithms minimize distance, so identical documents have distance 0 and unrelated ones approach 1.
Configure agglomerative clustering: 3 clusters (one per topic), precomputed distance matrix, average linkage. Average linkage uses the mean distance between all pairs in two clusters.
fit_predict() runs the clustering and returns an array of cluster labels — one per document. Documents with the same label belong to the same semantic cluster.
Group documents by cluster label and print. The Python docs should cluster together, neural network docs together, and API docs together — automatic topic discovery.

animation_sequence.message: You can now deduplicate RAG corpora by semantic similarity and discover topics via agglomerative clustering — all powered by all-mpnet-base-v2 embeddings. Fewer duplicates means sharper retrieval and less wasted LLM context!

instruction.en: Build a `SemanticDeduplicator` class that detects and removes near-duplicate texts using word-frequency vectors as a stand-in for real embeddings. Implement `encode(text)` to return a word frequency dict (lowercase), `cosine_sim(a, b)` to compute cosine similarity between two frequency dicts, and `deduplicate(texts, threshold)` that returns only the unique texts (keeping the first occurrence when duplicates are found). A document is a duplicate if its cosine similarity to any already-kept document meets or exceeds the threshold.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import math

class SemanticDeduplicator:
    def encode(self, text: str) -> dict:
        # Return word frequency dict, all lowercase
        # e.g., "hello world hello" -> {"hello": 2, "world": 1}
        pass

    def cosine_sim(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> float:
        # Compute cosine similarity between two word-frequency vectors
        # cosine_sim = dot(a,b) / (|a| * |b|)
        # Return 0.0 if either vector has zero magnitude
        pass

    def deduplicate(self, texts: list[str], threshold: float = 0.85) -> list[str]:
        # Return list of unique texts
        # For each text, if cosine_sim with ANY already-kept text >= threshold,
        # skip it (it's a duplicate). Otherwise keep it.
        pass

# Test:
dedup = SemanticDeduplicator()

# Exact encoding
print(dedup.encode("go go go"))

# Identical texts have similarity 1.0
v1 = dedup.encode("the cat sat")
v2 = dedup.encode("the cat sat")
print(round(dedup.cosine_sim(v1, v2), 1))

# Orthogonal texts have similarity 0.0
v3 = dedup.encode("alpha")
v4 = dedup.encode("beta")
print(round(dedup.cosine_sim(v3, v4), 1))

# Deduplication removes near-copies
result = dedup.deduplicate([
    "the quick brown fox",
    "the quick brown fox",
    "completely different sentence",
], threshold=0.99)
print(len(result))

en: encode: use text.lower().split() and count each word with a dict — increment counts for repeated words
cosine_sim: dot product is sum(a[k]*b[k] for shared keys). Magnitudes are sqrt(sum(v**2)) for each dict. Return dot/(mag_a*mag_b), or 0.0 if either magnitude is 0
deduplicate: maintain a list of kept embeddings alongside kept texts. For each new text, encode it and check cosine_sim against all kept embeddings. If all scores are below threshold, keep it

practice_challenges.test_code:
d = SemanticDeduplicator()
assert d.encode("hello world") == {"hello": 1, "world": 1}
assert d.encode("a a b") == {"a": 2, "b": 1}
assert d.encode("") == {}
v1 = d.encode("x y z")
v2 = d.encode("x y z")
assert abs(d.cosine_sim(v1, v2) - 1.0) < 0.001
assert d.cosine_sim({}, {"a": 1}) == 0.0
assert d.cosine_sim({"a": 1}, {}) == 0.0
assert d.cosine_sim({"a": 1}, {"b": 1}) == 0.0
r = d.deduplicate(["a b c", "a b c", "x y z"], threshold=0.99)
assert len(r) == 2
assert r[0] == "a b c"
assert r[1] == "x y z"
r2 = d.deduplicate(["a b", "a b c", "x y"], threshold=0.5)
assert len(r2) <= 3
r3 = d.deduplicate([], threshold=0.5)
assert r3 == []

instruction.en: Build a `TopicClusterer` class that groups texts into clusters based on word-frequency similarity. Implement `encode(text)` returning a word frequency dict (lowercase), `similarity_matrix(texts)` returning a 2D list of cosine similarities, and `cluster(texts, n_clusters)` that uses a simple greedy merge strategy: start with each text in its own cluster, repeatedly merge the two most similar clusters (using average similarity between all pairs of members), and stop when you have `n_clusters` clusters. Return a dict mapping cluster index to list of texts.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import math

class TopicClusterer:
    def encode(self, text: str) -> dict:
        # Return word frequency dict, all lowercase
        pass

    def _cosine_sim(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> float:
        dot = sum(a.get(k, 0) * b[k] for k in b)
        mag_a = math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in a.values())) if a else 0
        mag_b = math.sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in b.values())) if b else 0
        return dot / (mag_a * mag_b) if mag_a > 0 and mag_b > 0 else 0.0

    def similarity_matrix(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]:
        # Return NxN matrix where entry [i][j] = cosine_sim(texts[i], texts[j])
        pass

    def cluster(self, texts: list[str], n_clusters: int) -> dict:
        # 1. Encode all texts
        # 2. Start with each text in its own cluster: {0: [0], 1: [1], ...}
        # 3. While number of clusters > n_clusters:
        #    a. Find the two clusters with highest average pairwise similarity
        #    b. Merge them (add all members of one to the other, delete the empty one)
        # 4. Return {cluster_idx: [text strings]} for each remaining cluster
        pass

# Test:
tc = TopicClusterer()

# Similarity matrix basics
sim = tc.similarity_matrix(["a b", "a b", "x y"])
print(round(sim[0][1], 1))  # identical
print(round(sim[0][2], 1))  # orthogonal

# Clustering groups similar texts
clusters = tc.cluster([
    "python list tutorial",
    "python list comprehension",
    "java spring boot",
    "java spring framework",
], n_clusters=2)
print(len(clusters))

# Each cluster should have 2 members
sizes = sorted(len(v) for v in clusters.values())
print(sizes)

en: encode: split on whitespace, lowercase, count frequencies in a dict
similarity_matrix: create a list of encoded vectors, then compute _cosine_sim for every (i, j) pair in a nested loop

cluster: use a dict of {id: [indices]}. To find the best merge, compute average similarity between all pairs across two clusters. When merging, extend one cluster's list with the other's and delete the other

practice_challenges.test_code:
tc = TopicClusterer()
assert tc.encode("a b c") == {"a": 1, "b": 1, "c": 1}
sim = tc.similarity_matrix(["a", "a", "b"])
assert abs(sim[0][1] - 1.0) < 0.001
assert abs(sim[0][2] - 0.0) < 0.001
assert abs(sim[1][0] - sim[0][1]) < 0.001
c = tc.cluster(["a b", "a b c", "x y", "x y z"], n_clusters=2)
assert len(c) == 2
all_texts = []
for v in c.values():
    all_texts.extend(v)
assert len(all_texts) == 4
c1 = tc.cluster(["only one"], n_clusters=1)
assert len(c1) == 1
assert c1[list(c1.keys())[0]] == ["only one"]
